flashcards.front: What is a Class in Object-Oriented Modeling?
flashcards.back: A class is a blueprint/template that defines the attributes (data) and operations (behaviors) common to all objects of that type. It represents a group of objects with similar characteristics.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Association and Aggregation?
flashcards.back: Association is a general relationship where objects are connected. Aggregation is a special form of association representing 'has-a' relationship where the part can exist independently of the whole (e.g., Department has Professors).

flashcards.front: What is Composition in Class Modeling?
flashcards.back: Composition is a strong form of aggregation where the part cannot exist independently of the whole. If the container is destroyed, all contained objects are destroyed (e.g., Order contains OrderItems).

flashcards.front: What is Multiplicity in Class Diagrams?
flashcards.back: Multiplicity defines how many instances of a class can be associated with a single instance of another class. Notations include: 0..1 (optional), 0..* (many), 1..* (one or more), n (exactly n).
CRC stands for Class-Responsibility-Collaborator cards. They are index cards used during analysis to identify classes, their responsibilities, and their collaborations with other classes.

flashcards.front: What is Generalization in Class Modeling?
flashcards.back: Generalization (or Inheritance) is a relationship where a subclass inherits attributes and operations from a superclass. It represents an 'is-a' relationship (e.g., Student is a Person).

flashcards.front: What is Dependency in UML Class Diagrams?
flashcards.back: Dependency is a relationship where changes to one class may affect another class. It exists when a class uses another class as a parameter, local variable, or return type. Shown as dashed arrow.

flashcards.front: What are the three sections of a UML Class Box?
flashcards.back:
1. Top Section: Class Name (bold, centered)
2. Middle Section: Attributes (with visibility: +, -, #)
3. Bottom Section: Operations/Methods (with parameters and return type)

flashcards.front: What is Abstraction in Class-Based Modeling?
flashcards.back: Abstraction focuses on the essential characteristics of an object relative to the perspective of the viewer. It hides unnecessary details while showing only relevant attributes and behaviors.
Encapsulation is the bundling of data (attributes) and methods (operations) into a single unit (class). It also involves restricting direct access to some components to protect data integrity.

A qualified association uses a qualifier (key attribute) to identify specific instances of the associated class. For example, a Library has Members, and the member ID qualifies which member is being referenced.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Abstract Class and Interface?
flashcards.back: An abstract class can have both abstract methods (without implementation) and concrete methods; subclasses inherit from it. An interface only declares method signatures (no implementation) and a class can implement multiple interfaces.
